subject: ap-chemistry
title: Reaction Kinetics & Lewis Structures
stimulus:
6. At elevated temperatures, $NO_2$ undergoes decomposition in the gas phase, forming NO and $O_2$ as represented by the following equation.
$2\text{ NO}_2$(g) → $2\text{ NO}$(g) + $O_2$(g)
A scientist measures the change in [$NO_2$] over the first 100. s of the reaction at 546°C. The scientist uses the data collected from the experiment to generate the following two graphs.
Based on these data, the scientist makes the claim that the rate law for the reaction is rate = $k[\text{NO}_2]^2$.

parts.prompt: Explain how the graphs indicate that the reaction is second order with respect to NO .
parts.scoring_notes: The second graph plots 1/[NO₂] vs. time. A linear plot of 1/[A] vs. t is the diagnostic for second-order kinetics.

scoring_points.description: For a correct explanation citing the linearity of 1/[NO₂] vs. time: 1 point

required_elements: The plot of $1/[\text{NO}_2]$ vs. time is linear.
A linear $1/[A]$ vs. $t$ plot is the hallmark of a second-order reaction.
alternatives.correct_example: The graph of $1/[\text{NO}_2]$ vs. time is linear (constant slope), while the graph of $[\text{NO}_2]$ vs. time is not linear. For a second-order reaction, the integrated rate law is $\frac{1}{[A]_t} = kt + \frac{1}{[A]_0}$, predicting a linear $1/[A]$ vs. $t$ plot. The linearity of the $1/[\text{NO}_2]$ graph confirms second-order kinetics.

wrong_examples: Citing the [NO₂] vs. t graph as evidence — a curved [A] vs. t plot is consistent with any non-zero order.
Saying the slope is constant — that is just restating linearity.
common_traps: First order: linear ln[A] vs. t. Second order: linear 1/[A] vs. t. Zero order: linear [A] vs. t.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for a correct explanation: The plot of $1/[\text{NO}_2]$ versus time is the most linear, indicating that the reaction is second order with respect to \ce{NO2}.

sample_responses.response: The graph of $1/[\text{NO}_2]$ versus time is linear (straight line), while the graph of $[\text{NO}_2]$ versus time is curved. For a second-order reaction, the integrated rate law $\frac{1}{[A]_t} = kt + \frac{1}{[A]_0}$ predicts a linear $1/[A]$ vs. $t$ plot. The linearity of the $1/[\text{NO}_2]$ graph confirms second-order kinetics.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the linear $1/[\text{NO}_2]$ plot, connects it to the second-order integrated rate law, and contrasts it with the non-linear $[\text{NO}_2]$ plot. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The $[\text{NO}_2]$ vs. time graph curves downward, which means the reaction is second order.
sample_responses.commentary: A curved [A] vs. t plot is consistent with any non-zero order reaction, not specifically second order. The diagnostic for second order is the linearity of $1/[A]$ vs. t, which this response does not identify. No credit.

parts.prompt:
At a certain point in the reaction, the rate of disappearance of NO is determined to be 6.52 × 10 − 7 M/s.
Determine the rate of appearance, in M /s, of O at this same point in the reaction.

+
parts.scoring_notes: Rate of O₂ appearance = (1/2) × rate of NO₂ disappearance, from stoichiometry (2 NO₂ → 2 NO + O₂).

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated rate of O₂ appearance: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
From the stoichiometry $2\text{ NO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{ NO} + \text{O}_2$:
$\frac{d[\text{O}_2]}{dt} = -\frac{1}{2}\frac{d[\text{NO}_2]}{dt} = \frac{1}{2} \times 6.52 \times 10^{-7}\text{ M/s} = 3.26 \times 10^{-7}\text{ M/s}$

wrong_examples: Saying rate of O₂ = rate of NO₂ disappearance — stoichiometry ratio is 1:2 (O₂:NO₂).
Multiplying by 2 instead of dividing by 2.
common_traps: Rate of formation of O₂ = (coeff O₂ / coeff NO₂) × rate of disappearance of NO₂ = (1/2) × 6.52×10⁻⁷.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct calculated value: $6.52 \times 10^{-7}\text{ M/s} \times \frac{1\text{ mol }\text{O}_2}{2\text{ mol }\text{NO}_2} = 3.26 \times 10^{-7}\text{ M/s}$.

sample_responses.response: From stoichiometry $2\text{ NO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{ NO} + \text{O}_2$: the mole ratio of \ce{O2} to \ce{NO2} is 1:2, so rate of \ce{O2} appearance $= \frac{1}{2} \times 6.52 \times 10^{-7}\text{ M/s} = 3.26 \times 10^{-7}\text{ M/s}$.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies the stoichiometric mole ratio (1 mol O₂ per 2 mol NO₂) to convert the disappearance rate of NO₂ to the appearance rate of O₂. Earns the point.
Does not account for the stoichiometric ratio; 2 mol NO₂ are consumed for every 1 mol O₂ produced, so the O₂ rate must be half the NO₂ rate. No credit.

parts.prompt:
NO is a molecule that contains an odd number of electrons and can be oxidized to form the NO ion. In
NO , the unpaired electron is presumed to be localized on the nitrogen atom, as shown in the Lewis diagram in
the box on the left.
+
(i)In the box on the right, complete the Lewis diagram for NO . Be sure to show all bonding and
nonbonding electrons.
+
(ii)A student makes the claim that the bond angles in NO and NO are different from each other. Do
you agree or disagree with the student's claim? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: NO₂ has 17 electrons (odd); its geometry is bent (~134°). NO₂⁺ has 16 electrons (even); it is isoelectronic with CO₂ (linear, 180°). Part (c)(i): NO₂⁺ has a triple bond N≡O... no — NO₂⁺ has 16 electrons: linear with two double bonds (N=O). Part (c)(ii): agree — NO₂ is bent, NO₂⁺ is linear.

scoring_points.description: For a correct Lewis diagram of NO₂⁺ (16 electrons, linear, N=O double bonds): 1 point

required_elements: Structure: $[\text{O} = \text{N} = \text{O}]^+$ with each O having 2 lone pairs and N having no lone pairs.
alternatives.correct_example: $\text{NO}_2^+$: 16 valence electrons. Linear structure: $[:O=N=O:]^+$ where each O has 2 lone pairs and the formal charge on each O is 0 and N is 0 (or resonance structures).

wrong_examples: Drawing a bent NO₂⁺ with a lone pair on N — removing an electron from NO₂ removes the unpaired electron from N, leaving no lone pair and giving a linear molecule.
common_traps: NO₂ (17e⁻): bent, N has 1 unpaired electron. NO₂⁺ (16e⁻): linear, isoelectronic with CO₂.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct Lewis diagram of \ce{NO2+}: a linear structure with two double bonds (\ce{[O=N=O]+}), 2 lone pairs on each O, and no lone pairs on N.

sample_responses.response: [Draws \ce{NO2+} as [:O=N=O:]+, with 2 lone pairs on each O and no lone pairs or unpaired electrons on N — linear geometry.]
sample_responses.commentary: Removing the unpaired electron from NO₂ (17e⁻) gives NO₂⁺ (16e⁻). With no lone pair or unpaired electron on N, the molecule is linear (isoelectronic with CO₂). Correct structure earns the point.

sample_responses.response: [Draws \ce{NO2+} with a lone pair on N and a bent geometry, similar to NO₂.]
sample_responses.commentary: Removing one electron from NO₂ eliminates the unpaired electron from N. NO₂⁺ has no lone pair on N and is linear, not bent. No credit.

scoring_points.description: For 'agree' with correct VSEPR justification for different geometries: 1 point

required_elements: NO₂ is bent (bond angle ~134°) because N has one unpaired electron acting as a partial lone pair.
NO₂⁺ is linear (bond angle 180°) because N has no lone pairs — consistent with part (c)(i).
alternatives.correct_example: Agree. In NO₂, the nitrogen atom has an unpaired electron that acts like a partial lone pair, compressing the bond angle to ~134° (bent geometry). In NO₂⁺, the N atom has no lone pairs and no unpaired electrons. The two O atoms spread as far apart as possible, giving a linear structure with a 180° bond angle. Since the two molecules have different electron geometries, their bond angles differ.

wrong_examples: Disagree — the bond angles are indeed different between the bent NO₂ and linear NO₂⁺.
common_traps: An unpaired electron in VSEPR behaves like a lone pair and causes the angle to be less than 180°.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct answer and a valid justification, consistent with part (c)(i). Accept: (1) Agree. The angle of \ce{NO2+} is different from the angle in \ce{NO2} because there is no longer a nonbonding electron on the central atom in \ce{NO2+}, and the O atoms spread farther apart, forming a linear structure with a 180° bond angle. (2) Agree. The hybridization of N in \ce{NO2} is sp², giving ~120°; the hybridization of N in \ce{NO2+} is sp, giving 180°.

sample_responses.response: Agree. In \ce{NO2}, N has an unpaired electron that acts like a lone pair, pushing the O atoms closer together and producing a bent geometry with a bond angle of approximately 134°. In \ce{NO2+} (consistent with my Lewis diagram), N has no lone pairs or unpaired electrons, so the O atoms spread as far apart as possible — a linear geometry with a 180° bond angle.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies that the unpaired electron in NO₂ causes a bent geometry, and its absence in NO₂⁺ gives a linear geometry. References the Lewis diagram from part (c)(i). Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Disagree. Both \ce{NO2} and \ce{NO2+} have similar electron geometries so their bond angles are the same.
sample_responses.commentary: The student's claim is wrong — NO₂ (bent, ~134°) and NO₂⁺ (linear, 180°) have different electron geometries because of the unpaired electron on N in NO₂. No credit.
